title: Runtime Geometric Constraint Protocol Core Variables and Validity Gates for Locating Institutional Stall
author: Hunag, Jim Yongzhi
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description:
This technical note introduces a runtime-oriented analytical protocol for rule-dense institutional systems. It addresses a recurrent descriptive gap in classical jurisprudential and comparative frameworks: situations in which legal systems remain valid, recognized, and internally coherent, yet fail to advance under sustained, rule-consistent inputs. 
 Rather than proposing a normative theory, decision model, or computational method, the paper develops a minimal operational vocabulary for observing institutional non-advancement (“stall”) at the level of system interaction. Building on a cabinet–drawer distinction, it differentiates between domain-level rule coherence and system-level operability, showing why interpretive legal languages are often unable to localize breakdowns that arise at interfaces between institutional domains. 
 The protocol specifies how runtime states, handoff points, and accumulated load can be rendered descriptively visible without invoking causal explanations, policy judgments, or optimization goals. Its function is strictly analytical: to stabilize observation conditions so that operational blind spots in existing jurisprudential languages can be identified and compared. 
 This document is intended to operate downstream from validity-gating mechanisms and upstream from any computational, predictive, or policy-oriented models. It serves as an operational guide for locating where institutional processes cease to move, not as a tool for resolving or correcting those conditions.  The contribution is methodological and descriptive rather than evaluative, and is positioned as a protocol layer rather than a deployable system or application.
